ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Kueh Kasturi; mapped to the nearest parent class 'deep-fried snack food' (FOODON_03301710) as a defensible approximate anchor. A more specific term does not exist in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (fried dough-based snack) used as the closest defensible category. Kueh Kasturi is a deep-fried rice-flour and mung bean snack; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Kueh Kasturi or equivalent traditional Malay fried kueh as of 2026.

allergens: gluten (rice flour — low gluten but cross-contamination risk)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Kueh Kasturi is approximately 1 piece (40 g), as commonly sold at Malay kueh stalls and pasar malam in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Kueh Kasturi — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kueh Kasturi (cr_id F11090028), confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Flour and Mung Bean Based Snacks — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice flour-based fried snacks and mung bean products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kueh Kasturi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated from analogous deep-fried rice-flour snacks and mung bean-based kueh entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 15.28) / 100 ≈ 9.9, based on 40 g serving containing 15.28 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High estimated sugar content (~12 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite relatively low sodium (95 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Kueh Kasturi due to its high sugar load (estimated ~12 g/100 g) and low fibre content, which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and may elevate blood pressure indirectly; sodium content itself is moderate at 95 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (38.2 g/100 g) with medium-high GI (65), low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g), and estimated sugar ~12 g/100 g results in rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Kueh Kasturi; its high carbohydrate load (38.2 g/100 g), medium-high GI (65), and very low fibre (1.5 g/100 g) combine to produce a rapid and sustained postprandial glucose spike that is difficult to manage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.8 g/100 g from grated coconut and frying oil, combined with high sugar (~12 g/100 g) and deep-frying preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Kueh Kasturi sparingly, as the estimated saturated fat (~5.8 g/100 g from coconut) and high sugar content (~12 g/100 g) may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; deep-frying further increases total fat and caloric density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut (~5.8 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory; high estimated sugar (~12 g/100 g) approaches RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Kueh Kasturi; the saturated fat from grated coconut (~5.8 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6), and the high sugar content (~12 g/100 g) further exacerbates systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kueh Kasturi is a traditional Malay deep-fried snack popular in Singapore and Malaysia, made from a batter of rice flour, mung beans (green beans), grated coconut, egg, and sugar. The snack is formed into small oval or round shapes and deep-fried until golden, resulting in a crispy exterior with a slightly chewy, sweet interior. The grated coconut provides a characteristic richness and subtle tropical flavour, while the mung bean filling adds a mild, earthy sweetness. Kueh Kasturi is commonly sold at pasar malam (night markets), Malay kueh stalls, and festive occasions such as Hari Raya. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 246 kcal, 6.9 g protein, 7.3 g fat (of which approximately 5.8 g is saturated fat from coconut), and 38.2 g carbohydrate with only 1.5 g dietary fibre. The estimated sugar content is approximately 12 g/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 9.9 per 40 g serving. Clinically, this snack is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high carbohydrate and low fibre profile, and should be limited by those with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat and sugar content. Sodium is relatively low at 95 mg/100 g. Iron content is notable at 3.4 mg/100 g, likely contributed by the mung beans.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kueh kasturi?
What is kueh kasturi made of?
Is kueh kasturi suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of kueh kasturi?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11090028) with confidence 0.95. Sugar and saturated fat are estimated from typical Kueh Kasturi recipes as these fields are not explicitly reported in the HPB record; GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients from HPB; medium for sugar, saturated fat, and GI estimates.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Kueh Kasturi are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090028) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. sugar_g (12.0 g/100 g) is estimated from typical Kueh Kasturi recipes; not explicitly reported in the HPB source. 3. saturated_fat_g (5.8 g/100 g) is estimated from the grated coconut and frying oil contribution in typical Kueh Kasturi recipes; not explicitly reported in the HPB source. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable deep-fried rice-flour and mung bean snack entries; no direct GI measurement for Kueh Kasturi exists. 5. Glycemic load per serving = (65 × 15.28) / 100 ≈ 9.9, based on 40 g serving. 6. The dietary_tags field in the input includes 'plant_based'; this has been removed as Kueh Kasturi contains egg and is therefore not plant-based. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact codes exist for this traditional Malay snack as of 2026.
